title: Posture and Hand Shape
subtitle: A premium piano sound starts before the first note.
goal: Build bench position, relaxed shoulders, curved fingers, and a calm wrist.
warmup: Sit so the elbows are slightly in front of the torso.
Drop the hand onto five neighboring keys and let the fingers stay rounded.
lessonSteps: Play five-note patterns slowly with each hand alone.
Check that the wrist floats instead of collapsing.
Listen for even tone from finger 1 to finger 5.
practiceLoop: One minute right hand, one minute left hand, repeat three times.
Finish by playing both hands softly together.
coachNotes: Comfort creates consistency at the piano.
If the knuckles flatten, reset before repeating.
checkpoint: Both hands can play a five-note pattern with balanced tone.

title: Keyboard Geography
subtitle: Learn the map so reading and improvising become easier.
goal: Find all C, F, and G notes quickly and understand the black-key groups across the keyboard.
warmup: Point to every two-black-key group and name the C beside it.
Do the same for F near each three-black-key group.
lessonSteps: Play C, F, and G in several octaves with one finger.
Say the note name before touching the key.
Keep the eyes moving ahead of the hand.
practiceLoop: 60 seconds finding C, then F, then G.
Finish with a slow walk from low C to high C.
coachNotes: Fast orientation saves mental energy later.
Use landmarks, not guesswork.
checkpoint: You can locate C, F, and G anywhere on the keyboard on demand.

title: Five-Finger Control
subtitle: Make each finger useful instead of relying on the easy ones.
goal: Strengthen finger independence with five-note patterns in C and G position.
warmup: Play 1-2-3-4-5 and back down in one hand.
Keep every fingertip close to the keys.
lessonSteps: Repeat the pattern in both hands separately.
Play both hands together at a soft volume.
Accent finger 4 lightly to wake up the weaker side of the hand.
practiceLoop: Two minutes hands separate, two minutes hands together.
End with one ultra-slow clean pass.
coachNotes: Control beats force.
The quieter you can play evenly, the more control you have.
checkpoint: Both hands stay steady through a five-finger pattern with no finger collapse.

title: C Major Foundation
subtitle: Use the easiest key to teach motion and phrasing.
goal: Play the C major scale with correct fingering and shape one musical line from it.
warmup: Speak the fingering before you play.
Practice the thumb-under move slowly without tension.
lessonSteps: Play the scale up and down one octave in the right hand.
Repeat in the left hand with mirrored fingering awareness.
Finish with both hands one octave apart at a slow tempo.
practiceLoop: Three clean scale passes, then one pass with a small crescendo up and down.
Repeat the cycle twice.
coachNotes: Good fingering makes future keys easier.
Phrase the scale like a sentence instead of a drill.
checkpoint: You can play C major smoothly with reliable thumb turns.

title: Left-Hand Roots and Fifths
subtitle: Give the left hand a simple job it can do well.
goal: Build steady left-hand support with root-fifth patterns for basic chord progressions.
warmup: Play C and G as low single notes first.
Open the hand into root-fifth shapes slowly.
lessonSteps: Alternate root and fifth on whole notes.
Move through C, G, Am, and F without rushing the change.
Keep the left hand soft so it supports instead of dominates.
practiceLoop: Loop a four-chord progression for 3 minutes.
Record one take and check if the bass stays even.
coachNotes: A dependable left hand makes the whole arrangement sound more mature.
If the leap feels wide, look at the target before moving.
checkpoint: Your left hand can hold a root-fifth groove for one full progression.

title: Broken Chords for Ballads
subtitle: Turn static harmony into movement.
goal: Use simple broken-chord patterns in the right hand and left hand to create flowing accompaniment.
warmup: Play C-E-G slowly as separate notes.
Do the same with Am and F.
lessonSteps: Use a repeating 1-3-5 pattern in the right hand.
Anchor the left hand on single bass notes.
Switch chords every bar while keeping the pattern calm and even.
practiceLoop: Play 8 bars of broken chords without stopping.
Repeat once more with a softer touch.
coachNotes: Think smooth and connected, not percussive.
Broken chords sound premium when the rhythm is steady and the touch is gentle.
checkpoint: You can support a four-chord loop with a clean broken-chord texture.

title: Root Position Triads
subtitle: Build the three-note shapes behind most pop harmony.
goal: Memorize major and minor triads in root position and hear the emotional difference between them.
warmup: Play C major and A minor back to back.
Say root-third-fifth aloud.
lessonSteps: Build major triads on C, F, and G.
Build minor triads on Am, Dm, and Em.
Notice how the third changes the mood.
practiceLoop: Three rounds major set, then three rounds minor set.
Finish by mixing the triads randomly and naming them.
coachNotes: Naming the notes helps the ears catch up faster.
The third is the color note; listen for it.
checkpoint: You can build six common triads without guessing.

title: Pedal Basics
subtitle: Use sustain to add polish without creating mud.
goal: Learn when to change the sustain pedal and how to keep harmony clean.
warmup: Press and release the pedal silently a few times.
Play one chord and listen to how long the sound blooms.
lessonSteps: Change pedal right after the new chord lands.
Use short progressions and listen for blurred bass notes.
Combine light pedal with a slow ballad pattern.
practiceLoop: 8 bars no pedal, 8 bars with clean pedal changes, compare both.
Repeat until the change timing feels automatic.
coachNotes: The pedal should support the harmony, not hide sloppy changes.
When in doubt, use less pedal.
checkpoint: Your chord changes stay clear even with the pedal down.

title: Pop Comping I
subtitle: Make simple chords feel radio-ready.
goal: Use right-hand triads and left-hand bass notes to comp a common pop progression with groove.
warmup: Clap the rhythm pattern before you play it.
Play left-hand roots alone for one progression.
lessonSteps: Add right-hand chord stabs on beats 2 and 4.
Keep the left hand steady while the right hand changes rhythm.
Use short releases so the groove breathes.
practiceLoop: Play the progression for 16 bars.
Try one repeat with slightly stronger chorus accents.
coachNotes: Comping works when the rhythm is confident and uncluttered.
A short chord played in time sounds better than a long messy one.
checkpoint: You can comp a pop loop with clear left-right coordination.

title: Inversions for Smooth Flow
subtitle: Stop jumping more than the harmony needs.
goal: Use first and second inversions to keep chord progressions close and elegant.
warmup: Play C major in root position, first inversion, and second inversion.
Notice which notes stay close when moving to G and Am.
lessonSteps: Choose the nearest inversion for each chord in a progression.
Keep the top voice moving stepwise when possible.
Compare wide jumps versus smooth inversion-based movement.
practiceLoop: Play one progression with roots only, then with inversions.
Record both and hear the difference.
coachNotes: Smooth voice leading sounds expensive even at a basic level.
Let your eyes look for the smallest move, not the most familiar shape.
checkpoint: Your right hand can move through a progression with minimal jumping.

title: Major Scales: G and F
subtitle: Expand beyond C without losing confidence.
goal: Learn G major and F major scales with correct fingering and key awareness.
warmup: Review where F# and Bb live on the keyboard.
Play the key signatures alone before the full scale.
lessonSteps: Play G major hands separately, then F major hands separately.
Focus on fingering more than tempo.
End with one slow hands-together pass in each key.
practiceLoop: Two clean reps of G, two clean reps of F, then one alternating cycle.
Add a gentle crescendo toward the top note.
coachNotes: New keys feel easier when you know exactly which note changed.
Do not rush the thumb under in new keys.
checkpoint: You can play G and F major with reliable fingering and note awareness.

title: Natural Minor Sound World
subtitle: Hear the darker color and use it musically.
goal: Play A minor and D minor material while hearing how minor harmony shapes mood.
warmup: Play A natural minor slowly and listen to the 3rd, 6th, and 7th.
Alternate A minor and C major to compare color.
lessonSteps: Build Am, Dm, and Em triads.
Use a simple broken-chord pattern in A minor.
Create one short left-hand-right-hand texture that feels reflective.
practiceLoop: 8 bars of A minor groove, 8 bars of D minor groove.
Finish by improvising with only A minor scale notes.
coachNotes: Minor music is not just sad; it is focused and colored differently.
Keep the sound connected and intentional.
checkpoint: You can play a short minor-key texture that sounds stylistically clear.

title: Syncopation and Offbeat Chords
subtitle: Teach the right hand to sit around the beat instead of always on it.
goal: Develop offbeat chord placement and simple syncopation for modern accompaniment.
warmup: Clap rests and offbeats before touching the keyboard.
Play bass notes on the beat and chords on the and of 2 and 4.
lessonSteps: Use a two-chord loop and keep the pattern light.
Do not fill every gap; let the syncopation speak.
Add one anticipatory chord before the bar line.
practiceLoop: Two minutes clapping, then three minutes playing.
Record one pass and check whether the groove still feels stable.
coachNotes: Syncopation should feel playful, not rushed.
If it sounds late or messy, simplify the pattern.
checkpoint: Your offbeat chords land confidently against the pulse.

title: Lead Sheet Reading Basics
subtitle: Read symbols and build a usable part fast.
goal: Translate chord symbols into clean voicings and a simple accompaniment pattern in real time.
warmup: Name the chord quality before you play it.
Practice quick triad building from symbols only.
lessonSteps: Read a four-chord lead sheet and choose one voicing for each chord.
Add a left-hand root or root-fifth pattern.
Keep the rhythm simple so symbol reading stays the main skill.
practiceLoop: Play the chart three times through.
On the second pass, use inversions for smoother movement.
coachNotes: A useful reading pass beats a fancy one that falls apart.
Look one symbol ahead whenever possible.
checkpoint: You can turn a basic chord chart into a playable accompaniment.

title: Arpeggio Ladders
subtitle: Train the hands to climb harmony cleanly.
goal: Use one- and two-octave arpeggios to improve hand shaping, fingering, and harmonic awareness.
warmup: Play C, Am, F, and G arpeggios separately.
Notice which fingers begin and end each shape.
lessonSteps: Keep the arm moving smoothly over the thumb crossings.
Use a metronome and stay relaxed through the turn.
Finish by linking arpeggios into a pop progression.
practiceLoop: Two clean reps per arpeggio, then two progression loops.
End with a softer expressive pass.
coachNotes: Arpeggios are about flow more than force.
The wrist should help the hand travel, not freeze.
checkpoint: Your arpeggios move continuously without jerky thumb turns.

title: Sus and Add Chord Color
subtitle: Make simple harmony sound more contemporary.
goal: Add suspended and add9 colors to familiar progressions without overcrowding the arrangement.
warmup: Play a plain C chord, then Csus2, then Cadd9.
Listen to how the color changes without losing the root feeling.
lessonSteps: Apply sus2, sus4, and add9 colors to two chords in a progression.
Resolve suspended chords on purpose so the tension means something.
Keep the voicings light and singable.
practiceLoop: Loop one progression and test one color at a time.
Choose the two colors that sound most musical and keep only those.
coachNotes: Color tones work best when they are chosen, not sprayed everywhere.
A little color goes a long way in pop and worship styles.
checkpoint: You can upgrade a basic progression with tasteful suspended or added tones.

title: Gospel-Style Walkups
subtitle: Add energy between chords with confidence.
goal: Use passing bass movement and simple right-hand voicings to create a gospel-influenced transition feel.
warmup: Play bass notes C-D-E under a held C harmony.
Feel the difference between target chords and passing tones.
lessonSteps: Use a walkup into a IV chord and a V chord.
Keep the right hand simple so the bass motion is clear.
Resolve each walkup cleanly to avoid sounding random.
practiceLoop: Practice two walkups for 3 minutes each.
Finish with a short eight-bar progression that uses both.
coachNotes: Passing tones should lead somewhere.
If the bass line is strong, the right hand can stay elegant and simple.
checkpoint: You can use a short walkup that sounds intentional and stylistic.

title: Reading Rhythm Stacks
subtitle: Coordinate tied notes, rests, and layered patterns.
goal: Improve rhythmic reading by combining held notes in one hand with moving figures in the other.
warmup: Count tied notes aloud before playing.
Practice the left-hand rhythm alone first.
lessonSteps: Add the right-hand figure only after the left hand feels secure.
Hold long notes fully while the other hand continues moving.
Use slow tempo and clear counting.
practiceLoop: Four repetitions hands separate, four repetitions hands together.
Then record one clean attempt.
coachNotes: Rhythmic reading improves when you respect every rest and tie.
Count with your voice until the body understands it.
checkpoint: You can keep one hand still while the other continues in time.

title: Accompaniment Arranging
subtitle: Choose the right texture for the song instead of one default pattern.
goal: Learn to switch between block chords, broken chords, pads, and rhythmic stabs based on section energy.
warmup: Play the same progression with block chords first.
Repeat it with a broken-chord texture.
lessonSteps: Assign one texture to verse and another to chorus.
Keep the left hand simpler when the right hand gets busier.
Make the chorus bigger without simply playing louder all the time.
practiceLoop: Create a 16-bar arrangement with verse and chorus contrast.
Repeat once and refine the texture choices.
coachNotes: Arrangement is about role and space.
The best texture is the one that supports the moment.
checkpoint: Your verse and chorus feel like different sections, not one repeated loop.

title: Performance Touch and Phrasing
subtitle: Pull the full sound together like a finished player.
goal: Combine touch, timing, voicings, pedal, and dynamics into one polished performance study.
warmup: Play one simple progression and shape the top note as a melody.
Test soft, medium, and full chorus dynamic levels.
lessonSteps: Use pedal only where it adds bloom.
Balance the right-hand melody above the accompaniment.
Perform the full arrangement twice and choose the stronger take.
practiceLoop: One repair pass on the weakest phrase, then one full uninterrupted take.
Write down one musical choice that improved the performance most.
coachNotes: Polish comes from balance and intention.
Every section should have a reason for sounding the way it does.
checkpoint: You can deliver one short piano performance with shape, balance, and control.
